Vancouver Pilot OSM Can-BICS Classification

Colin.Ferster@ubc.ca

10/22/2020

Pilot city: Vancouver

Street data by OpenStreetMap Contributors (OSM) were classified according to Can-BICS definitions.

Methods overview:

  1. Ground reference data were collected using street-level imagery and aerial imagery (and other corroborating evidence where available). We collected a stratified random sample based on infrastructure types in open data provided by cities. The data were randomly split into training and testing subsets (70%/30%). Only the training data are presented here, the testing data are withheld for final accuracy reporting.
  2. Queries to classify OSM tags were developed using the training ground reference data.
  3. All OSM street and trail data (i.e. polyline features on OSM called “ways” with the highway tag) in the region were classified using the queries.
  4. An accuracy assessment was completed using the training ground reference data.

Map

  • Ground reference data points are represented by point markers.
    • Correct classifications are marked with check marks.
    • Incorrect classifications are marked by an X.
  • Roads with bicycling infrastructure are shown in colour.
  • Zoom in and click on the map features for more detail.

Classification accuracy assessment

The preliminary in-sample accuracy assessment was based on the training sample (a random selection of 70%) of the ground data. The testing sample (30%) were held back for the final accuracy assessment.

Definitions for the accuracy assessment:

  • Precision: The probability that a classified feature actually belongs to the class. Also known as user’s accuracy. Calculated as the proportion of classified positives that are true positives.
  • Recall: The proportion of the class that is being detected in the classificaiton. Also known as producer’s accuracy. Calculated as the proportion of classified positives out of the count of all positives in the ground reference data.
  • Prevalence is the proportion of the class in the ground reference data.

Two-hundred and eight ground reference points were used to refine queries and the preliminary in-sample accuracy assessment (Table 1 and Table 2). Local street bikeways had the best classification performance (indicated by a relatively high precision and recall, near 90%). Painted bike lanes and multi-use paths had a low precision (errors of commission, where other features are mistakenly included in the class), but a higher recall (most of these features were included in their correct class). In contrast, bike paths had a high precision (most features classified as bike paths were actually bike paths) but low recall (many bike paths were not detected as such). The none category had a high precision (most places mapped as none do not have any infrastructure), but low recall (some places with no infrastructure were included in other classes). Each of these classes are interpreted in more detail below.

Table 1. Classification matrix for pilot OSM Can-BICS classification of Vancouver. Reference counts are in columns and classification counts are in rows.
Bike Path Cycle Track Local Street Bikeway Multi-Use Path Painted Bike Lane None Total
Bike Path 10 2 0 2 0 0 14
Cycle Track 6 24 1 3 1 0 35
Local Street Bikeway 0 0 47 0 0 2 49
Multi-Use Path 4 7 0 16 0 8 35
Painted Bike Lane 0 0 0 0 16 15 31
None 2 0 5 0 1 36 44
Total 22 33 53 21 18 61 208
Table 2. Classification accuracy statistics by class.
Bike Path Cycle Track Local Street Bikeway Multi-Use Path Painted Bike Lane None
Precision 0.71 0.69 0.96 0.46 0.52 0.82
Recall 0.45 0.73 0.89 0.76 0.89 0.59
Prevalence 0.11 0.16 0.25 0.10 0.09 0.29

Interpretation

Bike paths where bikes are separated from all other modes of travel are a relatively recent development and were inconsistently tagged on OSM. Bike paths were often incorrectly identified as multi-use paths and cycletracks.

Cycle tracks were sometimes misclassified as multi-use paths (e.g., along the Stanley Park Causeway). This was mostly due to incorrect or non-specific attribution on OSM (i.e. “bike-only” wasn’t tagged on the northbound side of the Stanley Park Causeway).

Local street bikeways had the highest precision and recall. A big improvement in accuracy was achieved by including bicycle route data (collections of streets that belong to bicycle routes). Most errors were errors of omission where local street bikeways were classified as none. This was often a result of incorrect speed limit data (where OSM incorrectly attributed streets as having speed limits of 50 km/h that actually have 30 km/h limits and traffic calming features).

Multi-use paths were sometimes confused with bike only paths or cycle tracks (mostly errors of commission i.e. other types of infrastructure were incorrectly classified as multi-use paths). Additionally, some gravel multi-use paths (not meeting Can-BICS standards) do not have a surface tag on OSM.

Painted bike lanes had a low precision and a high recall. That means that the main issue is errors of commission (other types of features classified as painted bike lanes).

Misclassifications arose due to data errors on OSM.

  • OSM is missing the painted lane on King Edward Avenue.The painted bike lane on King Edward Avenue is not well connected (so it’s a low priority for community mapping)
  • OSM falsely identifies a painted lane on 16th Avenue. 16th Avenue is an informal low-comfort bike route to access UBC since there’s space for bicycling in the parking lane.
  • The north section of Main street was mapped as having a painted lane, and it does have sections of painted lane in some places, but where the samples were collected it is a shared lane on a major road (not meeting Can-BICS standards).

None includes areas with no infrastructure and they were sampled to include places where misclassification was likely. We did not include major roads since they can be evaluated with a simple visual inspection. Therefore the reported accuracy is lower than the true value (the vast majority of roads have no bike infrastructure). Our measurement only reflects places with no infrastructure that are likely to be misclassified.

None had a low recall meaning that there were errors of omission (places that had no infrastructure were included in other classes). For example, multi-use paths with gravel surfaces (not meeting Can-BICS standards), when they are missing a surface tag are classified as multi-use path.

There were errors for painted bike lines, as discussed above (OSM does not include recent painted bike lanes on King Edward or Nanaimo Streets - These are recently built and poorly connected painted lanes on major roads).

Geographically the main area for improvement is along the Seawall. Despite the misclassifiactions, the OSM mapping is generally indicative of the mix of multi-use and bike-only paths - high and medium comfort infrastructure - that exists there. The other location is along the Stanley park causeway where a cycle track is misclassified as a multi-use trail.

OSM data in Vancouver could be improved by:

  • Better indicating where bicycles and pedestrians are separated using the “segregated” tag.
  • Better indicating the surface of multi-use trails, especially where they are unpaved.
  • Including up-to-date speed limits on local streets and mapping traffic calming and diversion features.

Overall accuracy

The overall accuracy was 72% and the kappa value was 0.65 (where 0 is no better than random and 1 is a perfect classification) (Table 3). The reported overall accuracy does not account for weighting by stratification (the prevalence of each class). Additionally, the none category does not measure all places with no infrastructure (only those likely to be misclassified). Therefore, it’s more useful to focus on accuracy statistics for the individual classes at this point.

Table 3. Overall classification accuracy statistics.
Statistic Value
Accuracy 0.72
Kappa 0.65
AccuracyLower 0.65
AccuracyUpper 0.78
AccuracyNull 0.29
AccuracyPValue 0.00
McnemarPValue NaN
